audit_log.check: Autonomy vs Automation: PASS (intelligent structure decisions, not templates)
Consciousness Awareness: PASS (subject/hook/body mapped to 6D Neuro-Box)
Quality Standards: PASS (scannable, clear CTA, no clickbait)
No Manipulation: PASS (real urgency only, benefit-focused CTAs, no bait-and-switch)
Natural Language: PASS (conversational voice, scannable structure)
Transition Integration: PASS (hypnotic flow via Copy Transitions Master)

dependency.notes: Copy Transitions Master provides hypnotic flow patterns; integrated via transition validation in Python tools

consciousness_profile.description: Provides structural foundation for all email types; consciousness targeting happens via content selection within this structure

component.activation_strategy: BOTTOM foundation (not threatening) + LEFT curiosity (emotional hook)
component.consciousness_purpose: Get past spam filters and cognitive filters; signal relevance without alarm

balance_rules.rule: BOTTOM_SAFE before LEFT_SPECIAL: Subject must feel safe before being intriguing

component.activation_strategy: LEFT emotional connection (story, question) + RIGHT logical validation (deliver on subject promise)
component.consciousness_purpose: Validate subject line promise; hook attention with emotional or intellectual engagement

balance_rules.rule: Must deliver on subject line (no bait-and-switch)
LEFT_RIGHT balance: emotion + logic together (within 2-3 sentences)

component.activation_strategy: RIGHT logical clarity (short paragraphs, scannable) + TOP elevation (why this matters)
component.consciousness_purpose: Deliver on email promise; build understanding or belief; maintain singular focus

balance_rules.rule: Scannable structure (2-4 sentence paragraphs, white space)

component.activation_strategy: TOP benefit-focused (what they GET) + BACK social validation (others succeeded)
component.consciousness_purpose: Clear next step; benefit-focused; single action (no competing CTAs)

balance_rules.rule: Single CTA per email (no competing options)
Benefit-focused language (what they get, not what you want)
Easy action (direct link, no multi-step process)

component.activation_strategy: BOTTOM reassurance (risk reversal, guarantee) + BACK community/identity (who does this)
component.consciousness_purpose: Catch skimmers; reinforce main point or CTA; add bonus value or proof

balance_rules.rule: Reinforces main CTA or adds bonus insight

consciousness_profile.convection_notes: Well-structured emails increase forwards, shares, and replies; clear CTAs drive measurable action; scannable format reduces friction

purpose.primary: Define structural standards for all email types: subject lines, hooks, body, CTAs, P.S.
purpose.secondary: Integrate Copy Transitions Master for hypnotic flow between email components
purpose.tertiary: Provide validation tools for email structure quality (subject length, clickbait detection, CTA clarity, scannability)

when_to_use.scenario: Writing any email (welcome, nurture, promo, launch, lifecycle)
Reviewing email drafts for structural quality
Building email templates or swipe files

not_for.item: Subject line optimization alone (see KB-EMAIL-SUBJECT-LINES for deep dive)

principle.name: Singular Focus = Clarity = Action
principle.statement: One core idea, one primary CTA per email. Multiple topics or CTAs dilute and confuse.
principle.rationale: Cognitive load theory: decision paralysis with >1 choice; email is a single-shot attention medium

principle.name: Subject Line Honesty = Trust Compound
principle.statement: Subject line must deliver on promise in opening hook. No bait-and-switch, ever.
principle.rationale: Violating subject promise destroys trust permanently; deliverability tanks; unsubscribes spike

principle.name: Scannable > Beautiful
principle.statement: Short paragraphs (2-4 sentences), generous white space, conversational voice. Email is read on phones, while distracted.
principle.rationale: 60%+ opens on mobile; skimmers = 80% of readers; dense blocks = immediate delete

principle.name: Benefit-Focused CTAs
principle.statement: CTA language emphasizes what THEY get, not what YOU want. Make it easy, make it clear.
principle.rationale: Reader-first orientation; reduces friction; increases click-through 2-3x vs command language

principle.name: Real Urgency or None
principle.statement: Deadlines must be genuine. No fake scarcity, no manufactured countdown timers.
principle.rationale: Fake urgency kills trust permanently; constitutional violation (consent_intent)

principle.name: Hypnotic Flow via Transitions
principle.statement: Smooth transitions between email components create "hypnotic flow" - reader pulled through naturally, not forced.
principle.rationale: Copy Transitions Master provides 8 categories of transition patterns; reduces drop-off; increases read-through

requirements.req: No clickbait (misleading, exaggerated, withheld info for manipulation)
Delivers on promise in opening hook (no bait-and-switch)
Specific enough to repel non-relevant readers (filtering mechanism)
Passes "Would I open this?" test (sender-blind evaluation)

standard.formulas: See frameworks section for 6 proven formulas

requirements.req: Validates subject line promise immediately
Uses question, story opening, or bold statement
References previous email if in sequence ("Yesterday I told you...")
Hooks attention without gimmicks
Short paragraphs: 2-4 sentences maximum
Generous white space: line breaks between paragraphs
Conversational voice: write like talking to smart friend
Singular focus: one core idea per email
Scannable: bullets/lists where appropriate
Mobile-friendly: looks good on phone (60% of opens)
Single CTA: no competing options
Benefit-focused: what they GET by taking action
Explicit instruction: "Click here to..." "Reply and tell me..." "Sign up for..."
Easy action: direct link, no multi-step process
Clear next step: reader knows exactly what to do

standard.optional_reinforcement: Can repeat CTA in P.S. for skimmers

standard.when_to_use: Optional; use when adding value or reinforcing CTA for skimmers

tactical_uses.use: Reinforce main point or CTA (skimmers read P.S. first)

requirements.req: Brief (1-3 sentences max)
Adds value or reinforces (doesn't introduce new topic)

guardrail.rule: No misleading, exaggerated, or curiosity-gap-only subject lines

examples.bad: "You won't BELIEVE what happened..." (withholds info, hype)

guardrail.rule: Subject line promise MUST be delivered in opening hook
guardrail.violation_example: Subject: "How to double opens" → Hook: "Today I want to talk about engagement rates..." (different topic)

guardrail.rule: Urgency/scarcity language ONLY if deadline/limit is real
guardrail.prohibited: Fake countdown timers, manufactured scarcity, evergreen "closing soon"
guardrail.allowed: Real deadlines, actual inventory limits, legitimate time-bound offers

guardrail.rule: All emails must be mobile-readable (60%+ of opens)
guardrail.requirements: Subject ≤60 chars, paragraphs ≤4 sentences, generous white space

guardrail.rule: One core idea, one primary CTA per email
guardrail.remedy: If multiple ideas, create sequence instead of cramming into one email

framework.name: 6 Subject Line Formulas
framework.purpose: Proven patterns for getting emails opened without clickbait

examples.ex: "The counterintuitive truth about email frequency"
"The weird reason your emails go to spam"
"The 3 AM mistake killing your open rates"
"How to write emails people actually read (in 20 min/week)"
"How to launch without a huge list"
"How to sell without sounding salesy"
"Losing 10k subscribers taught me this about email"
"A 2am panic attack changed how I think about launches"
"My worst product flop revealed the secret to email sales"

formula.when_to_use: Story emails, personal narrative, origin stories
formula.neuro_activation: LEFT (emotional hook via story) + SPECIAL (personal relevance)

examples.ex: "Ready to fix your email strategy?"
"Want to see how Sarah doubled her list in 60 days?"
"Tired of emails that get ignored?"
"3 subject lines that tripled my opens"
"7 welcome email mistakes (and how to fix them)"
"5 minutes to better email copy"
"Daily emails vs weekly: which wins?"
"What I thought about email vs what actually works"
"Email marketing myths vs truths"

selection_heuristics.rule: IF email is story-based THEN use Formula 3 (story hook)
IF email is tactical/how-to THEN use Formula 2 (benefit-driven) or Formula 5 (numbered)
IF email challenges common belief THEN use Formula 6 (contrast) or Formula 1 (curiosity gap)
IF email seeks engagement/replies THEN use Formula 4 (question)
IF unsure THEN default to Formula 1 (curiosity gap) - safest, most versatile

framework.name: Hook Pattern Library
framework.purpose: Opening 1-3 sentences that validate subject and hook attention

pattern.structure: Start with specific moment/scene that validates subject line

examples.ex: Subject: "Losing 10k subscribers taught me this" → Hook: "It was 2am when I saw the unsubscribe spike. 10,147 people gone in 3 days."
Subject: "The 3 AM mistake killing your opens" → Hook: "I used to schedule all my emails for 3am. Seemed smart—be first in inbox. Turns out, terrible idea."

pattern.when_to_use: Story emails, personal narrative, case studies

pattern.structure: Ask question that gets mental "yes" and leads to body content

examples.ex: Subject: "Ready to fix your email strategy?" → Hook: "Have you ever hit 'send' and immediately regretted it?"
Subject: "Want to see how Sarah doubled her list?" → Hook: "What if you could grow your email list by 100% in 60 days without running ads?"

pattern.when_to_use: Engagement emails, curiosity hooks, qualifying readers

pattern.structure: Make contrarian or surprising claim that demands explanation

examples.ex: Subject: "Daily emails vs weekly: which wins?" → Hook: "Sending daily emails actually INCREASED my open rates. Here's why."
Subject: "Email marketing myths vs truths" → Hook: "Everything you think you know about email is probably wrong."

pattern.structure: Reference previous email to maintain narrative continuity

examples.ex: "Yesterday I told you about the 3am mistake. Today, I'll show you what to do instead."
"Remember Sarah from my last email? Here's what happened next."

selection_heuristics.rule: IF subject is story-based THEN use story_opening
IF subject is question THEN use question_opening
IF subject is contrarian THEN use bold_statement
IF email is part of sequence THEN use sequence_reference

framework.name: Body Structure Templates
framework.purpose: Proven structures for different email types

structure.step: Main Insight: The core teaching (3-5 paragraphs with examples)
Application: How to use this (1-2 paragraphs)
CTA: Next step (click, reply, sign up)
Hook: Start story at compelling moment
Conflict: What went wrong / challenge faced
Resolution: How it was solved / insight gained
Lesson: What reader should take away
CTA: How to apply or go deeper

template.when_to_use: Origin stories, case studies, personal narratives

structure.step: Agitate: Why status quo doesn't work
Proof: Evidence it works (testimonial, results, logic)

template.when_to_use: Product launches, promotional emails, sales pages

structure.step: Hook: Question or statement that gets mental "yes"

selection_heuristics.rule: IF email purpose is nurture/education THEN use educational template
IF email purpose is story-sharing THEN use story template
IF email purpose is promotional THEN use promotional template
IF email purpose is engagement/replies THEN use engagement template

examples.ex: "Click here to get the complete guide"
"Reply and tell me your biggest email challenge"
"Sign up to save your spot"
"Click to see what Sarah did next"
"Watch the 2-minute demo"
"Read the full case study here"
"Join before doors close Friday"
"Claim your spot (only 12 left)"
"Register by midnight to save $200"

cta_pattern.when_to_use: Launch close, limited offers (ONLY if deadline/limit is real)
cta_pattern.guardrail: CRITICAL: Deadline/limit MUST be real (no fake urgency)

examples.ex: "Hit reply and let me know"
"Click here to learn more (no signup required)"
"Check it out here"

cta_pattern.when_to_use: Cold subscribers, early relationship, engagement emails

selection_heuristics.rule: IF relationship is new/cold THEN use low_friction CTA
IF email is story/case study THEN use curiosity_driven CTA
IF real deadline exists THEN use urgency_driven CTA
IF unsure THEN default to benefit_focused CTA

anti_patterns.bad: Multiple competing CTAs in same email
Vague CTAs ("Click here" with no benefit)
Fake urgency ("Offer expires soon" but evergreen)

framework.name: P.S. Strategic Uses
framework.purpose: Optional component that adds value or reinforces for skimmers

use_case.structure: P.S. Brief reminder of main CTA + benefit
use_case.example: P.S. Remember, doors close Friday. Join now to get lifetime access.

use_case.structure: P.S. Additional tip or insight related to main content
use_case.example: P.S. Quick tip: The best time to send welcome emails is within 5 minutes of opt-in. Immediate beats delayed every time.
P.S. "This framework doubled my open rates in 3 weeks." - Sarah M.

use_case.structure: P.S. Guarantee or risk reversal reminder
use_case.example: P.S. 60-day money-back guarantee. If it doesn't work for you, full refund, no questions asked.

selection_heuristics.rule: IF email is promotional THEN use cta_reinforcement or risk_reversal
IF email is educational THEN use bonus_value
IF credibility is key concern THEN use social_proof
IF skimmers are majority (>80%) THEN always include P.S. to reinforce main point
IF email is short (<300 words) THEN P.S. optional (less needed)

framework.name: Copy Transitions Integration (Hypnotic Flow)
framework.purpose: Smooth reader movement between email components using 8 transition categories

transition.purpose: Subject creates curiosity → Hook delivers on promise while maintaining engagement
transition.example: Subject: "The 3 AM mistake" → Hook: "I used to schedule emails for 3am. Seemed smart. Turns out..."

transition.purpose: Hook sets up → Body explains "Here's why" or "Here's how"
transition.example: Hook: "I used to schedule for 3am." → Body: "Here's why that's a terrible idea..."

transition.purpose: Body builds understanding → CTA provides clear next action
transition.example: Body ends with insight → "So here's what to do next:" → CTA

transition.purpose: CTA asks for action → P.S. reinforces or adds value for skimmers
transition.example: CTA: "Join now" → P.S. "Remember, doors close Friday + 60-day guarantee"

categories.cat: 5. Escalation Bridges (and here's the kicker, but wait)
7. Explanation Bridges (let me explain, here's how)
8. Next Step Indicators (so what do you do, here's what to do next)

transition_categories_reference.integration_method: Python tool validates transition density and category variance across email

validation_via_python.note: See Layer 3 Python Tools for implementation

inputs.input: Email type (welcome, nurture, promo, launch, engagement)
Relationship stage (new subscriber, engaged reader, buyer)
Email sequence position (standalone, part of series)

ai_decisions.decision: Determine if P.S. needed and which type (Framework 5)

outputs.output: Email structure strategy (which frameworks to apply)

step.name: Generate Subject Line Options
step.process: Apply selected formula(s) to create 3-5 subject line options

validation.check: Passes "Would I open this?" test (human judgment)

step.output: 3-5 subject line options ranked by expected performance

step.name: Craft Opening Hook
step.process: Write 1-3 sentence hook that validates subject line and engages

validation.check: Delivers on subject line promise (no bait-and-switch)
Uses selected hook pattern from Framework 2

step.name: Structure Body Content
step.process: Apply selected body template (Framework 3) to organize main content

validation.check: Smooth transitions (Python tool: calculate_transition_density - pending KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS)

step.output: Body content (300-1000 words depending on type)

step.name: Create Clear CTA
step.process: Apply selected CTA pattern (Framework 4) to create benefit-focused call-to-action

step.name: Add P.S. (If Appropriate)
step.process: Determine if P.S. adds value; if yes, apply selected use case (Framework 5)

validation.check: Reinforces or adds value (doesn't introduce new topic)

step.name: Final Structure Validation
step.process: Run all Python validation tools on complete email
step.quality_gate: All tools must pass thresholds (see Layer 3 for tool specs)
step.output: Validation report + email ready for review/send

rule.condition: IF email is part of multi-email sequence
rule.action: THEN use sequence_reference hook pattern + maintain narrative continuity
THEN use low_friction CTA + avoid urgency language
THEN use P.S. for CTA reinforcement or risk reversal

rule.condition: IF subject line creates curiosity gap
rule.action: THEN hook MUST deliver on promise in first 1-2 sentences (no bait-and-switch)
THEN ensure extra scannability (more white space, bullets, subheads)

rule.condition: IF CTA asks for high commitment (purchase, application)
rule.action: THEN include risk reversal in body or P.S. (guarantee, refund policy)
THEN use story_opening hook + story body template + curiosity_driven CTA
THEN use benefit_driven or numbered subject + educational body template + benefit_focused CTA
THEN verify deadline/limit is REAL before using urgency_driven CTA (constitutional guardrail)

rule.condition: IF transition density is low (<0.5 transitions per 100 words)
rule.action: THEN add transition phrases using KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS categories to improve flow

tool.name: validate_subject_length
tool.purpose: Validate subject line is ≤60 characters for mobile display

case.input: "The comprehensive guide to email marketing strategies that actually work in 2025"

tool.name: detect_clickbait
tool.purpose: Detect clickbait patterns in subject lines using regex pattern matching
tool.validation_logic:
import re
def detect_clickbait(subject_line): """ Detects clickbait patterns using regex matching.
Clickbait indicators: - Excessive punctuation (!!!, ???) - All caps words (>2 in subject) - Withheld information ("You won't believe...", "This will shock...") - Exaggerated language ("AMAZING", "SHOCKING", "UNBELIEVABLE") - Manipulation phrases ("Don't open this unless...", "Only read if...")
Returns: dict: { 'is_clickbait': bool, 'patterns_detected': list, 'severity': str (none/low/medium/high), 'recommendation': str } """ patterns = [] severity_score = 0
# Pattern 1: Excessive punctuation if re.search(r'!!!|!{2,}|\?\?\?|\?{2,}', subject_line): patterns.append('excessive_punctuation') severity_score += 2
# Pattern 2: All caps words (count words >3 chars that are all caps) all_caps_words = re.findall(r'\b[A-Z]{4,}\b', subject_line) if len(all_caps_words) >= 2: patterns.append('excessive_caps') severity_score += 2
# Pattern 3: Withheld information phrases withheld_patterns = [ r"you won't believe", r"you'll never guess", r"this will shock", r"wait until you see", r"what happened next", r"the secret .* don't want you to know" ] for pattern in withheld_patterns: if re.search(pattern, subject_line, re.IGNORECASE): patterns.append('withheld_information') severity_score += 3 break
# Pattern 4: Exaggerated language exaggerated_words = [ 'AMAZING', 'SHOCKING', 'UNBELIEVABLE', 'INCREDIBLE', 'INSANE', 'MIND-BLOWING', 'REVOLUTIONARY', 'BREAKTHROUGH' ] for word in exaggerated_words: if word in subject_line.upper(): patterns.append('exaggerated_language') severity_score += 2 break
# Pattern 5: Manipulation phrases manipulation_patterns = [ r"don't open this unless", r"only read if", r"not for everyone", r"you need to see this" ] for pattern in manipulation_patterns: if re.search(pattern, subject_line, re.IGNORECASE): patterns.append('manipulation_phrase') severity_score += 3 break
# Determine severity if severity_score == 0: severity = 'none' elif severity_score <= 2: severity = 'low' elif severity_score <= 5: severity = 'medium' else: severity = 'high'
is_clickbait = severity_score >= 3
result = { 'is_clickbait': is_clickbait, 'patterns_detected': patterns, 'severity': severity, 'severity_score': severity_score }
if is_clickbait: result['recommendation'] = f'Detected {severity} clickbait. Rewrite subject line to be specific and honest.'
return result
tool.test_cases: <case input='"You WON\'T BELIEVE what happened!!!"' expected_clickbait="true" expected_severity="high"/>

tool.name: analyze_hook_strength
tool.purpose: Analyze opening hook effectiveness using multiple factors
tool.validation_logic:
import re
def analyze_hook_strength(hook_text, subject_line): """ Analyzes opening hook effectiveness.
Scoring factors: - Length appropriate (20-100 words typical for 1-3 sentences) - Contains question or bold statement (engagement) - Uses "you" or "I" (personal connection) - Specific details (not generic) - Delivers on subject line promise
Returns: dict: { 'hook_score': float (0-10), 'strengths': list, 'weaknesses': list, 'delivers_on_subject': bool, 'recommendation': str } """ score = 5.0 # Start neutral strengths = [] weaknesses = []
word_count = len(hook_text.split())
# Factor 1: Length (20-100 words ideal for 1-3 sentences) if 20 <= word_count <= 100: score += 1.0 strengths.append('appropriate_length') elif word_count < 20: score -= 0.5 weaknesses.append('too_short') else: score -= 0.5 weaknesses.append('too_long')
# Factor 2: Engagement (question or bold statement) has_question = '?' in hook_text bold_indicators = ['I used to', 'Here\'s the truth', 'Let me tell you', 'It was'] has_bold_statement = any(indicator in hook_text for indicator in bold_indicators)
if has_question or has_bold_statement: score += 1.5 strengths.append('engaging_opening') else: weaknesses.append('lacks_engagement')
# Factor 3: Personal connection ("you", "I", "we") personal_pronouns = len(re.findall(r'\b(you|I|we)\b', hook_text, re.IGNORECASE)) if personal_pronouns >= 2: score += 1.0 strengths.append('personal_connection') else: weaknesses.append('impersonal')
# Factor 4: Specificity (numbers, names, specific details) has_numbers = bool(re.search(r'\d+', hook_text)) has_specific_details = len(hook_text.split()) > 15 and ('am' in hook_text.lower() or 'pm' in hook_text.lower() or 'when' in hook_text.lower())
if has_numbers or has_specific_details: score += 1.0 strengths.append('specific_details') else: weaknesses.append('too_generic')
# Factor 5: Delivers on subject line promise (keyword overlap check) subject_keywords = set(re.findall(r'\b\w{4,}\b', subject_line.lower())) hook_keywords = set(re.findall(r'\b\w{4,}\b', hook_text.lower())) keyword_overlap = len(subject_keywords & hook_keywords)
delivers_on_subject = keyword_overlap >= 1 if delivers_on_subject: score += 1.5 strengths.append('delivers_on_subject') else: score -= 2.0 weaknesses.append('bait_and_switch_risk')
# Cap score at 10 hook_score = min(score, 10.0)
result = { 'hook_score': round(hook_score, 1), 'strengths': strengths, 'weaknesses': weaknesses, 'delivers_on_subject': delivers_on_subject }
if hook_score < 6.0: result['recommendation'] = 'Hook needs improvement. Add engagement, personal connection, or specific details.'
return result

case.subject: "The 3 AM mistake killing your opens"
case.hook: "I used to schedule all my emails for 3am. Seemed smart—be first in inbox. Turns out, terrible idea."

tool.name: validate_cta_clarity
tool.purpose: Validate CTA is benefit-focused and clear
tool.validation_logic:
import re
def validate_cta_clarity(cta_text): """ Validates CTA clarity and benefit-focus.
Checks: - Contains action verb (click, reply, sign up, join, watch, read, etc.) - Contains benefit language (to get, to see, to receive, to save, etc.) - Not command-only ("Buy now!" without benefit) - Not vague ("Click here" without context) - Single CTA (not multiple competing options)
Returns: dict: { 'is_clear': bool, 'is_benefit_focused': bool, 'cta_score': float (0-10), 'issues': list, 'recommendation': str } """ score = 5.0 issues = []
action_verbs = [ 'click', 'reply', 'sign up', 'join', 'watch', 'read', 'download', 'register', 'get', 'claim', 'grab', 'see', 'learn', 'discover' ]
benefit_patterns = [ r'to get', r'to see', r'to receive', r'to save', r'to learn', r'to access', r'to claim', r'to discover', r'to unlock' ]
# Check 1: Contains action verb has_action_verb = any(verb in cta_text.lower() for verb in action_verbs) if has_action_verb: score += 2.0 else: score -= 2.0 issues.append('no_action_verb')
# Check 2: Contains benefit language has_benefit = any(re.search(pattern, cta_text, re.IGNORECASE) for pattern in benefit_patterns) if has_benefit: score += 2.5 is_benefit_focused = True else: is_benefit_focused = False issues.append('not_benefit_focused')
# Check 3: Not command-only (check for exclamation without benefit) command_only_patterns = [r'buy now!', r'subscribe!', r'join now!', r'act now!'] is_command_only = any(re.search(pattern, cta_text, re.IGNORECASE) for pattern in command_only_patterns) if is_command_only and not has_benefit: score -= 2.0 issues.append('command_only')
# Check 4: Not vague vague_patterns = [r'^click here$', r'^learn more$', r'^click$'] is_vague = any(re.search(pattern, cta_text.strip(), re.IGNORECASE) for pattern in vague_patterns) if is_vague: score -= 1.5 issues.append('too_vague')
# Check 5: Single CTA (check for "or" which often indicates multiple options) has_multiple = ' or ' in cta_text.lower() if has_multiple: score -= 1.0 issues.append('multiple_ctas')
# Check 6: Contains specific outcome has_specific_outcome = len(cta_text.split()) >= 5 if has_specific_outcome: score += 0.5
cta_score = max(0, min(score, 10.0)) is_clear = cta_score >= 6.0 and has_action_verb
result = { 'is_clear': is_clear, 'is_benefit_focused': is_benefit_focused, 'cta_score': round(cta_score, 1), 'issues': issues }
if cta_score < 6.0: recommendations = [] if 'no_action_verb' in issues: recommendations.append('Add clear action verb (click, reply, join, etc.)') if 'not_benefit_focused' in issues: recommendations.append('Add "to [benefit]" language (e.g., "Click to get the guide")') if 'too_vague' in issues: recommendations.append('Be specific about what they get') result['recommendation'] = ' | '.join(recommendations)
return result

case.input: "Click here to get the complete guide"

tool.name: calculate_scanability_score
tool.purpose: Calculate email scannability using paragraph/sentence metrics
tool.validation_logic:
import re
def calculate_scanability_score(email_body): """ Calculates email scannability score.
Factors: - Paragraph length (ideal: 2-4 sentences) - Sentence length (ideal: 10-20 words) - White space ratio (line breaks between paragraphs) - Use of bullets/lists
Returns: dict: { 'scanability_score': float (0-10), 'avg_paragraph_length': float, 'avg_sentence_length': float, 'white_space_ratio': float, 'uses_bullets': bool, 'issues': list, 'recommendation': str } """ score = 5.0 issues = []
# Split into paragraphs (double line breaks) paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in email_body.split('\n\n') if p.strip()] num_paragraphs = len(paragraphs)
# Split into sentences (rough approximation) sentences = re.split(r'[.!?]+', email_body) sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()] num_sentences = len(sentences)
# Calculate metrics if num_paragraphs > 0: avg_paragraph_length = num_sentences / num_paragraphs else: avg_paragraph_length = 0
total_words = len(email_body.split()) if num_sentences > 0: avg_sentence_length = total_words / num_sentences else: avg_sentence_length = 0
# White space ratio (line breaks vs total characters) line_breaks = email_body.count('\n') total_chars = len(email_body) white_space_ratio = line_breaks / total_chars if total_chars > 0 else 0
# Check for bullets uses_bullets = bool(re.search(r'[-•*]\s', email_body))
# Scoring # Factor 1: Paragraph length (ideal 2-4 sentences) if 2 <= avg_paragraph_length <= 4: score += 2.0 elif avg_paragraph_length > 6: score -= 1.5 issues.append('paragraphs_too_long') elif avg_paragraph_length < 1.5: score -= 0.5 issues.append('paragraphs_too_short')
# Factor 2: Sentence length (ideal 10-20 words) if 10 <= avg_sentence_length <= 20: score += 2.0 elif avg_sentence_length > 25: score -= 1.5 issues.append('sentences_too_long') elif avg_sentence_length < 8: score -= 0.5 issues.append('sentences_too_choppy')
# Factor 3: White space (ideal >0.03 ratio) if white_space_ratio >= 0.03: score += 1.5 else: score -= 1.0 issues.append('insufficient_white_space')
# Factor 4: Uses bullets (bonus for scannability) if uses_bullets: score += 0.5
scanability_score = max(0, min(score, 10.0))
result = { 'scanability_score': round(scanability_score, 1), 'avg_paragraph_length': round(avg_paragraph_length, 1), 'avg_sentence_length': round(avg_sentence_length, 1), 'white_space_ratio': round(white_space_ratio, 3), 'uses_bullets': uses_bullets, 'issues': issues }
if scanability_score < 6.0: recommendations = [] if 'paragraphs_too_long' in issues: recommendations.append('Break up long paragraphs (keep to 2-4 sentences each)') if 'sentences_too_long' in issues: recommendations.append('Shorten sentences (aim for 10-20 words each)') if 'insufficient_white_space' in issues: recommendations.append('Add more line breaks between paragraphs') result['recommendation'] = ' | '.join(recommendations)
return result

case.input:
"This is a paragraph.

This is another paragraph.

And a third one."

tool.name: calculate_transition_density
tool.purpose: Calculate transition phrase density for hypnotic flow (integrates KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS)

outputs.output: transitions_detected (list of dict with category + text)

tool.validation_logic:
import re
def calculate_transition_density(email_text): """ Calculates transition phrase density using 8-category system from KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS.
8 Transition Categories: 1. Curiosity Hooks (pull forward via gap) 2. Direct Address (you, I, we) 3. Reasoning Connectors (so, because, here's why) 4. Shared Understanding (mind-reading, empathy) 5. Escalation Bridges (and here's the kicker, but wait) 6. Proof Indicators (here's proof, for example) 7. Explanation Bridges (let me explain, here's how) 8. Next Step Indicators (so what do you do, here's what to do next)
Ideal density: 0.5-1.5 transitions per 100 words Ideal category variance: 4-6 unique categories
Returns: dict: { 'transition_density': float, 'transitions_detected': list, 'category_variance': int, 'flow_score': float (0-10), 'recommendation': str }
Note: Full implementation requires KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS-v1.0 integration """ # Placeholder implementation - will be replaced with full KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS integration
transition_patterns = { 'curiosity_hooks': [r'but here\'s the thing', r'and that\'s when', r'here\'s what I discovered'], 'direct_address': [r'\byou\b', r'\bI\b', r'\bwe\b'], 'reasoning_connectors': [r'\bso\b', r'\bbecause\b', r'here\'s why', r'\bthat\'s why\b'], 'shared_understanding': [r'you know that feeling', r'we\'ve all been there', r'sound familiar'], 'escalation_bridges': [r'here\'s the kicker', r'but wait', r'and here\'s the best part'], 'proof_indicators': [r'here\'s proof', r'for example', r'case in point', r'here\'s what happened'], 'explanation_bridges': [r'let me explain', r'here\'s how', r'here\'s what I mean'], 'next_step_indicators': [r'so what do you do', r'here\'s what to do', r'your next step'] }
transitions_detected = [] categories_used = set()
for category, patterns in transition_patterns.items(): for pattern in patterns: matches = re.finditer(pattern, email_text, re.IGNORECASE) for match in matches: transitions_detected.append({ 'category': category, 'text': match.group(), 'position': match.start() }) categories_used.add(category)
word_count = len(email_text.split()) transition_density = (len(transitions_detected) / word_count) * 100 if word_count > 0 else 0 category_variance = len(categories_used)
# Calculate flow score score = 5.0
# Factor 1: Density (ideal 0.5-1.5 per 100 words) if 0.5 <= transition_density <= 1.5: score += 2.5 elif transition_density < 0.3: score -= 2.0 elif transition_density > 2.0: score -= 1.0
# Factor 2: Category variance (ideal 4-6 categories) if 4 <= category_variance <= 6: score += 2.5 elif category_variance < 3: score -= 1.5 elif category_variance > 7: score -= 0.5
flow_score = max(0, min(score, 10.0))
result = { 'transition_density': round(transition_density, 2), 'transitions_detected': transitions_detected, 'category_variance': category_variance, 'flow_score': round(flow_score, 1), 'integration_status': 'Placeholder - Full integration pending KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS-v1.0' }
if flow_score < 6.0: if transition_density < 0.5: result['recommendation'] = 'Add more transition phrases to improve flow (aim for 0.5-1.5 per 100 words)' elif category_variance < 4: result['recommendation'] = 'Use more diverse transition categories (aim for 4-6 unique categories)'
return result

integration_notes.note: Full transition pattern library will be imported from KB-COPY-TRANSITIONS-v1.0
Category definitions and scoring thresholds will align with Copy Transitions Master
Tool will validate transition usage against 8-category taxonomy
